projects.title: Retail KPI Storytelling
projects.impact: Delivered a repeatable analytics path: RFM segments for customer prioritization, YTD/MTD KPI views for recurring review, and charts framed for decisions rather than exploratory notebooks alone.
projects.problem: The retail dataset needed a clear view of who buys, what sells, and how performance moves month-to-month - not a pile of raw retail tables.
projects.summary: Formation Data & Business Intelligence project on a retail dataset covering Sales, Product, Customer, and Store tables. I built the full analytical path from relational modeling and advanced SQL through cleaning, RFM segmentation, time-based KPIs, and visual storytelling for business questions.
projects.tagline: Sales, customers, and stores turned into RFM segments and YTD/MTD KPIs.
projects.approach: Modeled the four-table retail schema, wrote advanced SQL (joins, CTEs, window functions, views), cleaned and transformed sales data in Python, built RFM customer segments, and designed YTD/MTD KPI views with Matplotlib and Seaborn so trends were scannable.
highlights: Queried a 4-table retail schema with joins, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, views, and stored procedures.
Built RFM customer segments and Year-to-Date / Month-to-Date KPI views from cleaned sales data.
Used pandas and NumPy for exploration, then Matplotlib and Seaborn to present trends teams could act on - hierarchy of KPIs first, detail second.
projects.roleBody: Owned modeling, advanced SQL, transformation, and KPI storytelling across Sales, Product, Customer, and Store tables.
projects.goal: Turn a multi-table retail dataset into a clear, repeatable view of who buys, what sells, and how performance moves month to month.

projects.title: Trusted Test Data Reporting
projects.impact: Moved reporting from ad-hoc manual collation to a repeatable JIRA-backed workflow with clearer anomaly visibility and faster turnaround for cross-functional reviews.
projects.problem: Cross-functional engineering teams needed faster, trustworthy answers from high-volume test and diagnostic data - manual collation slowed decisions and hid anomalies.
projects.summary: At Capgemini Engineering and KPIT Engineering, I analyzed and validated large Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, automated collection workflows in JIRA, and delivered clear reports for hardware, software, and systems teams.
projects.tagline: Large validation datasets cleaned, tracked, and turned into decisions.
projects.approach: Validated large-scale HiL and system test datasets, automated data collection and defect tracking in JIRA, and wrote Python scripts to generate result data and reporting for hardware, software, and systems teams.
highlights: Validated large-scale Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, surfacing performance trends and anomalies for engineering teams.
Automated data collection and defect-tracking workflows in JIRA to improve traceability and cut analysis turnaround.
Built Python scripts to generate and analyze result data, improving reporting accuracy for hardware, software, and systems teams.
projects.roleBody: Analyzed and validated large Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, automated collection and defect tracking, and built clearer anomaly visibility for engineering teams.
projects.goal: Give cross-functional engineering teams faster, trustworthy answers from high-volume test and diagnostic data.

projects.title: Chetek Sports | Tennis Referee Companion
projects.impact: A researched UX package spanning why, market claim, competitive landscape, survey prompts, persona Walid Chetek, and high-fidelity flows for welcome, auth, referee management, schedule, and live scoring.
projects.problem: Arbiters still juggle physical rulebooks, static ITF/ATP/WTA references, and third-party summaries mid-match. Finding and interpreting a rule under time pressure is slow, easy to get wrong, and never designed for the court.
projects.summary: Vivez votre match comme les pros. The Tennis Referee Companion is a native mobile product for tennis arbiters in France: faster rule confidence, calmer match control, and live scoring built for the chair, not for spectators. UX and research by Amani Medini. Development by Ilyes.
projects.tagline: UX-driven mobile app for tennis arbiters: scoring, rules, and live match control.
projects.approach: Market research, competitive analysis, client-led survey questions, persona work, then wireframes, hi-fi French UI, and prototype validation for auth, schedule, match setup, and live court scoring.
highlights: Started from a real arbiter frustration: juggling multiple rule resources during matches.
Competitive scan covered official rulebooks, third-party summaries, and general officiating apps.
Persona Walid Chetek: Marseille arbiter/coach who needs searchable rules, offline access, and quick reference.
Hi-fi French UI for welcome, auth, tirage au sort, schedule sheets, and tap-friendly live scoring.
projects.roleBody: Freelance UX: research, competitive analysis, survey synthesis, personas, flows, and high-fidelity French UI. The brief came from a tennis arbiter client who lived the problem on court. Development by Ilyes.
projects.goal: Give French-speaking tennis arbiters a user-friendly mobile companion: searchable rules thinking, offline resilience, and match tools from tirage au sort through live scoring so they can officiate with confidence.

metrics.label: Research artifacts
metrics.detail: Why, market research, competitive analysis, user survey, and persona.

metrics.label: Core product flows
metrics.detail: Welcome, auth/recovery, tirage au sort, match settings, schedule, live scoring.

metrics.label: Built for France
metrics.detail: Native iOS direction with French copy for arbiters on court.

sections.title: Why this exists
sections.body:
This did not start as a generic sports idea. It started with a tennis arbiter describing the same pain over and over: too many resources, too little calm when a rule has to be found now.\n\nThe product is more than a digital rulebook. It is a streamlined companion so arbiters can handle the court with confidence.

The research pack sat beside concrete deliverables: research plan, interviews, competitive analysis, personas, wireframes, style guide, and feedback analysis.

images.alt: Why I created this application research slide
images.caption: The spark: an arbiter tired of juggling resources mid-match.

images.alt: Tennis app case study overview slide with scope and role
images.caption: Scope card: France, native iOS, Dec 2022 to Jul 2023.

images.alt: Chetek Sports overview slide with deliverables and welcome auth phones
images.caption: Deliverables pack: research artifacts, wireframes, style guide, and early auth screens.

sections.title: The claim and the problem
sections.body: The claim was simple: tennis arbiters need a more efficient, user-friendly way to manage the complexity of the rules.\n\nPhysical rulebooks are cumbersome in a live match. Specific rules are hard to find fast. Jumping between books and online materials creates confusion and errors.

images.alt: Market research slide with claim and problem
images.caption: Market research: claim, problem, and the cost of slow reference.

sections.title: What arbiters use today
sections.body: There was no dedicated arbiter companion owning this space. Official ITF, ATP, and WTA rulebooks are comprehensive but static and awkward mid-point. Third-party summaries are thin and often unofficial. General sports officiating apps rarely go deep enough for tennis edge cases.\n\nThat gap is the design opportunity: depth of the official sources, speed of a mobile tool, built for the chair.

images.alt: Competitive analysis of rulebooks and officiating apps
images.caption: Competitive analysis across rulebooks, summaries, and general officiating apps.

sections.title: Listening on court
sections.body: Inputs came from the client arbiter and the difficulties officials share when applying rules live.\n\nThe survey pressed on current resources, speed of lookup, unclear interpretations, must-have features, and how critical offline access is when connectivity dies at a venue.

images.alt: User survey questions for tennis arbiters
images.caption: Survey prompts that shaped the feature priorities.

sections.title: Meet Walid
sections.body: Walid Chetek is 42, based in Marseille: tennis arbiter for 5 years, coach for 10, comfortable on a phone but still stuck with bulky rulebooks when the point is hot.\n\nHe wants searchable official rules, offline access, quick reference, scenario learning, and is open to tools like a digital coin toss. His frustrations are speed, interpretation clarity, and the absence of an app built for arbiters.

images.alt: Persona card for Walid Chetek tennis arbiter
images.caption: Persona: Walid Chetek, tennis arbiter in Marseille.

sections.title: The product on the table
sections.body:
Once the research landed, the hi-fi system had to read as one product: welcome, auth, schedule, referee setup, and live scoring in the same navy and lime language.

This board is the handoff snapshot: French microcopy, court tools, and tournament structure in one frame.

images.alt: Isometric collage of CHETEK Sports LiveScore final screens
images.caption: Final screens board: welcome through live scoring.

sections.title: First serve
sections.body: The product had to feel like court light from frame one: rackets, ball, and a promise for people running the match.\n\nWelcome stays quiet. Login, register, or continue as a guest. No fan clutter.

images.caption: Welcome: Vivez Votre Match Comme Les Pros.

sections.title: Getting onto court
sections.body: Auth had to be boring in the best way. Arbiters should not fight forms before a match.\n\nLogin, register, forgot password, verification, reset, success. French copy end to end.

images.alt: French authentication and password recovery screens
images.caption: Auth and recovery as one calm strip.

sections.title: Before the first point
sections.body: Match control starts before the first ball. Tirage au sort, who serves, who sits left of the chair, ball change, format, court overview.\n\nFewer taps between ready and live, including the coin-toss style choice Walid was open to.

images.alt: Tirage au sort, match parameters, court overview
images.caption: Toss, parameters, then court overview.

sections.title: The day sheet
sections.body: Tournament days are lists before they are points: epreuves, poules, club vs club, singles and doubles slots.\n\nThe schedule layer keeps the arbiter oriented in the competition structure without leaving the product voice.

images.alt: Tournament epreuves and match sheet screens
images.caption: From championship sheet to S1 through D2.

sections.title: Live on court
sections.body:
Live scoring is the loudest product idea: a court you can tap, with Ace, Filet, Faute, and F. de pied in reach while the point is alive.\n\nDark and light themes, ball-change reminders, readable set columns. Research had to become muscle memory here, not another settings page.

Across states, the active court side flips with the point so Ace, Filet, Faute, and F. de pied stay on the hand that needs them.

images.alt: Live scoring screens with court zones and rule actions
images.caption: Live scoring under time pressure.

images.alt: Four live scoring states showing court side flips and set progression
images.caption: Scoring states: active court side flips as the point moves.

sections.title: Handing it over
sections.body: Research slides, persona, wireframes, style thinking, and hi-fi flows went to build with Ilyes. The collaboration focus was French microcopy, scoring states, and keeping court interactions honest in implementation.

projects.nextSteps: Keep testing with arbiters after launch, deepen offline rule lookup and scenario learning, and tighten live scoring edge cases from real tournament feedback.

projects.title: Friends of Figma (FoF) Tunisia
projects.impact: 290 members on the official FoF network; hosted Tunisia Config Watch Party 2026 and recurring meetups; speaking at DevFest, GDG MENAT Summit, and Women Techmakers.
projects.problem: Tunisia lacked an official Figma-affiliated community space for designers and builders to learn together in public.
projects.summary: I founded and lead Friends of Figma (FoF) Tunisiaiaia, listed on the official Friends of Figma network as Tunisia’s chapter for Africa. As Figma Community Advocate and organizer, I grow a local space for designers and builders through meetups, Config watch parties, speaker programs, and learning in public - the same communication craft I bring to data work.
projects.tagline: Official Friends of Figma Tunisia chapter - 290 members and growing.
projects.approach: Founded and organized FoF Tunisiaiaia: meetups, Config watch parties, speaker programs, and partnerships with WTM and GDG - connecting design practice with clear public storytelling.
highlights: Official organizer of Friends of Figma Tunisia (290 members on friends.figma.com/tunisia).
Hosted Tunisia Config Watch Party 2026 and meetups such as Vibe Right with Figma and Ramadhan Bytes: Design Edition.
Connect design and data storytelling through talks at DevFest, GDG MENAT Summit 2025, and Women Techmakers.
projects.roleBody: Founded and lead Friends of Figma Tunisia: meetups, Config watch parties, speaker programs, and partnerships with WTM and GDG.
projects.goal: Build an official Figma-affiliated community space in Tunisia where designers and builders learn together in public.

speaking: Friends of Figma Tunisia - Config Watch Party 2026 & speaker programs

timeline.text: Started co-organizing Google Developer Group meetups and became a Women Techmakers Ambassador.
Qualified Top 10 at the AI Artathon during the Global AI Summit in Riyadh.
Product Owner at UTIK - translating stakeholder needs into data-informed product specs and backlog priorities.
Completed a Master’s in Electronics (Microelectronics & Instrumentation) at Monastir University. Joined Capgemini Engineering.
Founded Friends of Figma (FoF) Tunisiaiaia - Tunisia’s only Figma-affiliated community chapter.
Completed Women Developer Academy. Presented a Capgemini Tech Talk and kept growing community programs.
Spoke at DevFest and GDG MENAT Summit in Dubai. Continued data validation, reporting, and storytelling work at KPIT Engineering.
Completed Formation Data & Business Intelligence (SQL, Python, RFM, visualization). Hosted Tunisia Config Watch Party 2026 with Friends of Figma Tunisia. Joined Technovation WTM Media Council.

aboutPage.bio: I'm Amani, a Data Analyst in Ariana, Tunisia. I analyze complex datasets and build KPI narratives with SQL and Python, with a designer's eye for clarity.
aboutPage.title: About me in 2 minutes

vision.body: I have a clear plan for growth in the coming years: deepen analytical craft while continuing to lead with design clarity. I want to shape narratives from data and from product experience, make sharper decisions, and drive design thinking across teams. I aspire to lead, foster learning in public, and build work that people trust and enjoy using.
vision.title: Vision

aboutPage.bioDesign: I'm Amani, a UX-minded designer and Friends of Figma Tunisia advocate. I shape brand storytelling, mobile product flows, and research-led interfaces across freelance client work.

experience.title: E-Powertrain Software Tester
bullets: Analyzed and validated large-scale Hardware-in-the-Loop (HiL) test datasets for ECUs, BMS, and Relay Boxes, identifying performance trends and system anomalies.
Automated data collection and defect-tracking workflows in JIRA, improving data traceability and cutting analysis turnaround time.
Extracted, cleaned, and interpreted calibration and diagnostic data using INCA to support parameter optimization and reporting.

experience.title: Automotive Software Tester
bullets: Built Python-based automation scripts to generate and analyze test-result data for E/E vehicle systems (BMW Gen6, G2X platforms), improving data accuracy and reporting reliability.
Consolidated and analyzed validation datasets spanning BMS, wiring, ECUs, and CAN networks, converting raw results into clear, actionable reports for engineering teams.
Tracked and reported defect and test-coverage metrics in JIRA, supporting data-driven decisions across hardware, software, and systems teams.

experience.title: Product Owner
bullets: Analyzed product requirements and team input to define structured, data-informed functional specifications.
Prioritized product backlog using data on technical risk, system dependencies, and delivery milestones.
Coordinated cross-functional teams to align data-related deliverables with business objectives.

workProcess.body: Understand business goals and project requirements with clients, then run user research including interviews, surveys, analytics, and competitor analysis. Identify pain points and motivations through persona creation.
workProcess.title: Research & discovery

workProcess.body: Map content and navigation with sitemaps and user flows. Prioritize features that align with user needs and product goals so the structure stays usable and purposeful.
workProcess.title: Information architecture & planning

workProcess.body: Craft low-fidelity wireframes to shape layout and functionality, then bring designs to life with interactive prototypes for testing and validation.
workProcess.title: Wireframing & prototyping

workProcess.body: Enhance the interface with branding, typography, and color while keeping accessibility and consistency. Add micro-interactions when they improve clarity and polish.
workProcess.title: UI design & visual development

workProcess.body: Conduct usability testing, gather feedback, and refine wireframes, prototypes, and visuals based on real user interactions.
workProcess.title: Testing & iteration

workProcess.body: Prepare clear handoff for engineering so the validated experience can be built with consistent components, states, and flows.
workProcess.title: Handoff & implementation

workProcess.body: Track behavior with analytics and heatmaps, gather feedback, and keep improving engagement through continuous design updates.
workProcess.title: Post-launch evaluation & optimization
